summary:
The Songze culture was a Neolithic culture of the Yangtze River Delta region in present-day Jiangsu and Shanghai, China, occupying roughly 3900–3300 BCE. It is recognized as a transitional culture between the earlier Majiabang culture and the subsequent [[event:1674|Liangzhu culture]], which succeeded it in the same geographic area. Songze communities practiced wet rice agriculture, kept domesticated pigs, and produced a wide range of pottery including distinctive narrow-necked vessels and tripod forms.

Excavations at the type site of Songze in suburban Shanghai have revealed stratified burials with social differentiation evidenced by the quantity and quality of grave goods, including jade ornaments and fine ceramics in elite graves versus simpler pottery in others. The Songze culture's ceramic and jade traditions contributed to the technological and aesthetic developments that would culminate in the remarkable craft achievements of the later Liangzhu culture, making it an important link in the cultural sequence of the Yangtze Delta.
